data.title: FIRSTSUN CAPITAL BANCORP SEC 10-K Report
data.description:
FirstSun Capital Bancorp, headquartered in Denver, Colorado, has released its 2024 Form 10-K report, providing a comprehensive overview of its financial and operational performance over the past year. The company, which operates as a financial holding company through its subsidiaries including Sunflower Bank, Logia Portfolio Management, and FEIF Capital Partners, has detailed its strategic initiatives, business highlights, and the challenges it faces in the current economic environment.

Financial Highlights

  • Net Interest Income: $296.9 million, an increase of $3.5 million or 1.2% compared to 2023, driven by higher interest income on loans.
  • Net Income: $75.6 million, a decrease from $103.5 million in 2023, primarily due to increased noninterest expenses and provision for credit losses.
  • Diluted Earnings Per Share: $2.69, compared to $4.08 in 2023, reflecting the decrease in net income.
  • Adjusted Net Income: $87.7 million, reflecting adjustments for non-recurring expenses such as terminated merger-related expenses.
  • Adjusted Diluted Earnings Per Share: $3.13, reflecting adjustments for non-recurring expenses.
  • Return on Average Total Assets: 0.96%, compared to 1.38% in 2023, indicating a decrease in asset efficiency.
  • Return on Average Stockholders' Equity: 7.56%, compared to 12.50% in 2023, reflecting lower profitability.
  • Net Interest Margin: 4.06%, a decrease from 4.23% in 2023, due to higher interest expense on liabilities.
  • Efficiency Ratio: 68.28%, compared to 59.81% in 2023, indicating higher noninterest expenses relative to revenue.

Business Highlights

  • Business Overview: FirstSun Capital Bancorp operates as a financial holding company providing a full spectrum of financial services through its subsidiaries, including Sunflower Bank, Logia Portfolio Management, and FEIF Capital Partners.
  • Geographical Performance: Sunflower Bank operates across Texas, Kansas, Colorado, New Mexico, Arizona, California, and Washington, with a mortgage lending platform extending to 43 states. The bank's headquarters was relocated to Dallas, Texas, in November 2023.
  • Business Strategy: The company focuses on organic growth by investing in people, technology, and infrastructure, aiming to build a premier regional bank. It emphasizes relationship-driven banking services and local decision-making in key Southwest and Western growth markets.
  • New Product Launches: In December 2024, FirstSun decided to rebrand its mortgage division, ceasing operations under the Guardian tradename and transitioning to Sunflower Bank Mortgage Lending.
  • New Production Launches: In February 2025, FirstSun received approval to establish two new depository branches in San Diego and Los Angeles, California, to support its expansion in Southern California.
  • Lending Activities: The company offers a range of lending services, including commercial and industrial loans, commercial real estate loans, residential real estate loans, public finance loans, and consumer loans. It participates in the SBA 7(a) program to support small businesses.
  • Mortgage Banking Activities: FirstSun's mortgage operations include full-service residential mortgage products and services, with a focus on purchase money mortgages. The company sells a majority of its originated residential mortgage loans to Fannie Mae, Freddie Mac, and private investors.
  • Wealth Management: The company provides a comprehensive suite of wealth management services, including private banking, wealth planning, investment management, trust, and retirement plan services.
  • Human Capital Resources: As of December 31, 2024, FirstSun employed 1,142 individuals, with a focus on fostering a diverse and inclusive work environment. The company offers competitive compensation and benefits, along with learning and development programs to support employee growth.
  • Future Outlook: FirstSun plans to continue its expansion strategy in Texas and Southern California, leveraging its expertise in mergers and acquisitions to evaluate strategic opportunities that enhance its market position and service offerings.

Strategic Initiatives

  • Liquidity Management: FirstSun Capital Bancorp is focused on maintaining a strong liquidity position and optimizing its capital structure. The company has implemented a liquidity management policy to ensure adequate liquidity and sources of available liquidity to meet both short-term and long-term demands. This includes ongoing analysis and monitoring of funding requirements, diversification of funding sources, and liquidity contingency planning.
  • Capital Management: FirstSun's capital management activities include maintaining a robust capital position with stockholders’ equity increasing to $1,041.4 million as of December 31, 2024, primarily due to net income and the issuance of common stock. The company did not pay dividends to common shareholders during 2024 or 2023. The Bank's liquidity is supported by customer deposits, FHLB advances, and principal and interest payments on loans and investment securities. The company has access to alternative funding sources, including the sale of loans, acquisition of non-core deposits, and borrowings through the Federal Reserve's discount window.
  • Future Outlook: FirstSun plans to continue its focus on maintaining a strong liquidity position and optimizing its capital structure. The company aims to meet future cash needs primarily through the generation of deposits and has a strategic focus on growing core deposits through relationship-based banking. The company also intends to manage its liquidity position to meet daily cash flow needs while balancing assets and liabilities to achieve stockholder return objectives. Additionally, FirstSun is committed to maintaining compliance with regulatory capital requirements and optimizing its capital structure to support future growth.

Challenges and Risks

  • Economic and Geographic Risks: The company faces significant economic and geographic-related risks, particularly due to its concentration in specific U.S. regions such as Texas, Kansas, Colorado, New Mexico, Arizona, California, and Washington. Economic downturns, rising interest rates, and recessions could lead to increased credit losses, higher deposit and funding costs, and a decline in demand for products and services. Inflationary pressures and Federal Reserve policies also pose risks, potentially leading to economic uncertainty and affecting the company's financial performance.
  • Trust and Wealth Management Risks: The trust and wealth management business is vulnerable to changes in economic and market conditions, which could lead to reduced fees and client attrition.
  • Lending Risks: The company is exposed to lending risks, particularly in commercial and industrial and commercial real estate lending, which involve higher credit risks and could lead to increased nonperforming assets and credit losses.
  • Interest Rate Risks: Changes in rates can affect the company's net interest income and the value of its assets and liabilities.
  • Operational Risks: The company faces operational risks, including potential losses due to errors, omissions, or fraudulent behavior by employees or third parties. Cybersecurity threats and technology failures could disrupt operations and harm the company's reputation.
  • Regulatory Risks: Regulatory risks are heightened due to the extensive regulation of the banking industry. Changes in laws and regulations could limit the company's activities and increase compliance costs.
  • Mergers and Acquisitions Risks: The company is also subject to risks related to mergers and acquisitions, which could disrupt business and dilute stockholder value.
  • Management Strategies: Management has identified the need to effectively manage credit risk and maintain adequate capital levels as critical to the company's success. Strategies to address these challenges include enhancing risk management practices and maintaining a strong core deposit base to support growth.
  • Market Risks: The company is exposed to market risks, including interest rate fluctuations and changes in the value of investment securities. Management actively monitors and manages these risks to mitigate potential adverse effects on the company's financial condition and results of operations.

SEC Filing: FIRSTSUN CAPITAL BANCORP [ FSUN ] - 10-K - Mar. 07, 2025

data.title: EQUITY BANCSHARES INC SEC 10-K Report
data.description:
Equity Bancshares Inc., a financial holding company headquartered in Wichita, Kansas, has released its 2024 Form 10-K report, showcasing significant growth in both financial and operational metrics. The company, which operates through its wholly-owned banking subsidiary, Equity Bank, has expanded its branch network and strengthened its market presence through strategic acquisitions and organic growth.

Financial Highlights

  • Net Interest Income: $186.2 million, driven by a 99 basis point increase in yields on interest-earning assets.
  • Net Income: $62.6 million, a substantial increase from $7.8 million in the previous year, primarily due to higher net interest income and non-interest income.
  • Basic Earnings Per Share: $4.04, reflecting the significant increase in net income.
  • Diluted Earnings Per Share: $4.00, consistent with the increase in net income.
  • Efficiency Ratio: 60.77%, improved from the previous year due to increased net interest income and non-interest income outpacing changes in non-interest expense.

Business Highlights

  • Company Overview: Equity Bancshares Inc. is a financial holding company headquartered in Wichita, Kansas, with a wholly-owned banking subsidiary, Equity Bank, operating 71 branches across Arkansas, Kansas, Missouri, and Oklahoma as of December 31, 2024.
  • Branch Network Expansion: The company has expanded its branch network from 2 to 71 branches since its inception, reflecting significant growth through strategic acquisitions and organic expansion.
  • Acquisition Strategy: The company has a history of acquiring and integrating community banks, with recent acquisitions including Rockhold BanCorp and KansasLand Bancshares, Inc. in 2024, which expanded its presence in Missouri and Kansas.
  • Employee Growth: The company has grown its team from 19 to 810 full-time equivalent employees as of December 31, 2024, supporting its expanded operations and customer service capabilities.
  • Commercial Banking Focus: The company primarily focuses on commercial banking, with commercial loans making up over 71.1% of its loan portfolio as of December 31, 2024.
  • Geographical Footprint: The company operates in both metropolitan and community markets, with a strategic focus on growing commercial loans in metropolitan areas like Kansas City, Wichita, and Tulsa, while leveraging stable core deposits from community markets.
  • Risk Management Practices: The company emphasizes strong risk management practices, including disciplined lending practices and comprehensive risk management systems to maintain asset quality.
  • Technological Investments: Significant investments have been made in information technology systems to support growth, enhance product offerings, and improve customer experience.
  • Future Outlook: The company plans to continue its strategy of consolidating community banks and maintaining organic growth, with a focus on enhancing the performance of acquired banks and expanding its commercial customer base.

Strategic Initiatives

  • Strategic Acquisitions: The company completed two mergers in 2024, acquiring Rockhold BanCorp and Kansasland Bancshares, Inc., which added significant deposits and expanded the company's geographic footprint. These strategic acquisitions are part of the company's ongoing efforts to integrate acquired banks into its operational platform, enhance stockholder value, and improve loan and deposit mix.
  • Capital Management: The company increased its total stockholders' equity by $140.1 million, driven by a common stock capital raise and net income of $62.6 million. It also repurchased $11.9 million in treasury stock and declared dividends of $8.7 million, or $0.54 per share, reflecting a 26% increase from the previous year. The company maintained a strong capital position with a Tier 1 Leverage Ratio of 11.67% and a Common Equity Tier 1 Capital Ratio of 14.51%.
  • Future Outlook: The company plans to continue pursuing strategic acquisitions and organic growth, leveraging its geographic footprint to access low-cost stable core deposits for funding commercial loan growth. It aims to enhance stockholder value through economies of scale and improved franchise efficiency. The company is also focused on maintaining sound asset quality and appropriate levels of capital reserves to ensure liquidity and meet regulatory capital requirements.

Challenges and Risks

  • Economic Risks: The potential for recessionary conditions could lead to increased nonperforming loans and reduced demand for products and services. Unstable global economic conditions, including trade policy changes and military conflicts, may adversely affect the company's business and financial performance.
  • Credit and Interest Rate Risks: The company is vulnerable to interest rate fluctuations, which could impact net interest income. The inability to effectively manage credit risk, particularly in a loan portfolio heavily secured by real estate, poses a significant threat. Additionally, the concentration of large loan relationships increases exposure to potential losses.
  • Operational Risks: The company relies heavily on its management team, and the unexpected loss of key officers could adversely affect operations. The development and use of AI present new challenges, including regulatory compliance and potential biases in AI models. Cybersecurity threats remain a significant concern, with the potential for unauthorized access to sensitive information.
  • Regulatory Risks: The company is subject to extensive regulation, which imposes additional costs and affects profitability. Changes in laws and government policies, particularly those related to consumer financial protection, could materially impact operations. The evolving regulatory landscape around environmental, social, and governance (ESG) practices may also impose new costs and risks.
  • Market Risks: The company is exposed to market risks, including fluctuations in commodity prices and interest rates. These factors could adversely affect financial condition and results of operations.
  • Management Strategies: Management's strategies to address these challenges include enhancing risk management frameworks, investing in technology to improve operational efficiency, and maintaining a strong capital position to support growth and absorb potential losses. The company is also focused on diversifying its loan portfolio and expanding its geographic presence to mitigate concentration risks.

SEC Filing: EQUITY BANCSHARES INC [ EQBK ] - 10-K - Mar. 07, 2025

data.title: Walker & Dunlop Prices Amended $450 Million Credit Agreement
data.description:
Walker & Dunlop priced an amended $450 million senior secured credit agreement.

The term loan under the amended agreement will initially pay interest at a rate equal to SOFR plus 2%, the company said Friday in a statement.

After the first full fiscal quarter ending after the closing date, the interest margin on the loan will be subject to a 25 basis points step down if Walker & Dunlop's total leverage ratio is equal to or less than 2-to-1.

The amended agreement will include a $50 million three-year revolving credit facility that will pay interest at a rate equal to SOFR plus 1.75%, the company said.

Proceeds from an offering of $400 million of notes due 2033 and the amended credit agreement will be to reduce the principal amount under a term loan agreement, among other things.

data.title: US to hold hearing on China's efforts to boost semiconductor industry
data.description:
The U.S. Trade Representative's Office will hold a hearing on Tuesday into older Chinese-made "legacy" semiconductors that could heap more U.S. tariffs on chips from China that power everyday goods from autos to washing machines to telecoms gear.

The probe, which was started under then-President Joe Biden in December, aims to protect American and other semiconductor producers from China's massive state-driven buildup of domestic chip supply. A 50% U.S. tariff on Chinese semiconductors started on Jan. 1.

Legacy chips use older manufacturing processes introduced more than a decade ago and are often far simpler than chips used in AI applications or sophisticated microprocessors.

The Commerce Department said in December that two-thirds of U.S. products using chips had Chinese legacy chips in them, and half of U.S. companies did not know the origin of their chips including some in the defense industry.

The hearing comes as President Donald Trump has called for the repeal of a $52.7 billion law to boost U.S. chips manufacturing that has made awards to Intel Samsung Electronics , Micron , TSMC and others.

The probe is being conducted under Section 301 of the Trade Act of 1974, the same unfair trade practices statute Trump invoked to impose tariffs of up to 25% on some $370 billion worth of Chinese imports in 2018 and 2019, triggering a nearly three-year trade war with Beijing.

The China Association of Automobile Manufactures told USTR it believes the Chinese auto chips industry "does not employ anti-competitive and non-market means to achieve its objectives."

The Foundation for American Innovation plans to argue China is exploiting America’s free-market system and free trade-oriented policies "to corner markets, undermine U.S. firms, and gain influence over key supply chains" and will warn of "the consequences of a failure to take action to address China’s subsidies and market distortions" on silicon carbide wafers.

The China Chamber of Commerce for Import and Export of Machinery and Electronic Products said in comments with USTR new tariffs "would lead to a higher US inflation rate and substantial harm to U.S. households, consumers, importers and exporters, and domestic companies in the industry."

data.title: Tech, Media & Telecom Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Technology, Media and Telecom. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1224 ET - Broadcom is guiding for a $60 million to $90 million AI silicon opportunity in 2027 from its three hyperscaler customers, which is a huge step up from the $15 billion to $20 billion addressable market it had last year. But it's still too early to have much conviction in that outlook and Broadcom's expected market share in 2027, given that it will likely face intense competition from Nvidia, Raymond James analysts say in a research note. There's also no guarantee that potential customers being courted by Broadcom will follow through on deploying high volumes of its application-custom ASIC chips for AI, they say. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

1056 ET - Broadcom has become a major AI play for investors and should hold that posture for years to come given the goals of its top customers, Benchmark analyst Cody Acree says in a research note. Its big hyperscaler customers, Google and Meta, seem to have been joined by ByteDance, and each of the three are planning to deploy up to a million clusters of XPUs--the chips used to train generative AI models--across a single fabric, or the interconnecting infrastructure for chips, the analyst says. The combination of just those three customers represents an estimated serviceable addressable market of $60 billion to $90 billion in fiscal 2027 across Broadcom's networking and XPU businesses, Acree says. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

1036 ET - Hewlett Packard Enterprise's guidance is particularly disappointing in light of revenue growth amid a recovery in the intelligent edge business, which focuses on analysis of data at the site where it's generated, and traditional/AI server growth, say BofA Securities analysts in a research note. A new cost-cutting initiative despite revenue growth also signals a much worse competitive pricing environment, which the analysts say they view as structurally bearish. However, the analysts also say that even with the weak signals for 2025, they view shares at 7x C26 EPS, "as too cheap to turn structurally negative." (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

1008 ET - Broadcom's strong quarterly report shows that despite potential headwinds in the AI space, including tariffs and DeepSeek, its customers are steadfast in investing to achieve what's been called artificial general intelligence, or AI that broadly exceeds human intelligence, Truist analysts say in a research note. If anything, the pace of innovation to achieve AGI seems to be accelerating, they say. Broadcom is in the middle of suppliers such as Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing who are accelerating wafer fabrication and packaging innovation and customers who are accelerating their rack-scale innovation, the analysts say. Broadcom adding four new hyperscalers as customers-to-be makes the acceleration even more evident, the analysts say. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

0945 ET - Broadcom is facing plenty of headwinds, particularly from the Trump administration's trade war and potential limits on AI chip exports, but the company seems confident that its dominance on custom chip sales should keep it moving in the right direction, Melius Research analysts say in a research note. It has a growing base of elite custom ASIC chip customers, they say. Broadcom makes a compelling argument that application-custom chips for AI will continue to proliferate, including specific accelerators of aspects of training and inferencing, the analysts say. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

0926 ET - Broadcom delivered a series of forecast beats in its fiscal 1Q report, but perhaps the biggest surprise was the announcement that it is working with four new hyperscaler customers in designing custom silicon, Melius Research analysts say in a research note. The four are aiming to be just as big as the existing three, which are already scaling to 1 million units by 2027 with a serviceable addressable market that year of $60 billion to $90 billion, the Melius analysts say. The new customers won't contribute to revenue for another couple years, but the biggest one could be OpenAI with a contribution in 2026, they say. Broadcom climbs 7% premarket. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

0858 ET - Hewlett Packard Enterprise's outlook is worrying as pricing discounts in its traditional server market compound with other challenges, say analysts at J.P. Morgan in a research note. The server and cloud-software company was already dealing with concerns about competition for AI servers and the impact of tariffs. Chief Financial Officer Marie Myers said pricing adjustments will be part of its mitigation strategy in light of the latest tariffs. "While the discounting-led headwinds the company is facing appear to be in some regards company-specific execution challenges... it raises concerns in relation to exacerbating an already aggressive pricing backdrop in the market which challenges profitability for not only HPE, but also peers," say the analystsShares tumble 17% to $14.82 premarket. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

0635 ET - Regulation of artificial intelligence should focus on the application of the technology rather than the technology itself, Mistral AI Chief Executive Arthur Mensch said in an interview at the Mobile World Congress in Barcelona. "When you're building a medical diagnostic assistant, you need to make sure that it's accurate enough," Mensch said. However, regulating the models behind the technology itself "makes little sense in that it's more or less a programming language," he adds. Mensch said Mistral AI is engaging with the European Commission, the EU's executive arm, on the application of the EU AI Act. (mauro.orru@wsj.com)

0242 ET - Universal Music Group's fourth-quarter results exceeded expectations and consensus estimates on revenue and profitability, Deutsche Bank Research analyst Silvia Cuneo says in a research note. The analyst maintains a positive long-term view for the record label, but lowers the more bullish 2025 estimates to reflect a more nuanced outlook discussed on the conference call, especially for streaming and merchandising, Cuneo says. Adjusted Ebitda forecast for 2026 is increased slightly thanks to a faster ramp-up of cost savings, she says. (nina.kienle@wsj.com)

2254 ET - An AI infrastructure investment is likely beginning in China's AI value chain, Nomura analysts say in a research note. Manus AI, a general AI agent, developed by China-based AI startup Monica, gained traction this week. According to its development team, the AI agent could complete complicated multi-stage tasks and outperform other mainstream agents. While its innovation may not be as groundbreaking as DeepSeek's, Nomura views the product as another example of China's accelerated AI innovation. The analysts think China's software makers could benefit from the AI investment upcycle in the mid- to long-term, as AI-driven efficiency improvements and cost reductions take effect. Meanwhile, software companies may adopt AI agents to further automate their platforms and improve user experience and engagement, leading to significant opportunities for business expansion, they add. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

2038 ET - The outlook for Malaysia's technology sector appears dim on weak 4Q 2024 earnings and a subdued 1Q this year, say Affin Hwang IB analysts, who downgrade the sector's rating to neutral from overweight. The AI-led semiconductor recovery has yet to benefit Malaysian tech players due to limited exposure, while the automotive sector, to which Malaysian companies have some exposure, also remains weak, analysts Kevin Low and Faustine Chua say. Geopolitical risks, potential U.S. tariffs, and rising costs further dampen sentiment and high PE valuations are becoming harder to justify, they say. They cut 2025-2026 earnings estimates for the sector by 15%-16%, with a sharp rebound unlikely. Affin Hwang prefers Frontken, Kelington Group, Inari Amertron, and SFP Tech for sector exposure. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

data.title: Financial Services Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Financial Services. Exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1218 ET - Gap's 4Q results proved to be solid, with the company's comparable sales being positive at three out of its four brands, Jefferies analysts say in a research note. The apparel retailer has made steps to improve its position under its management team, the analysts say. However, there is growth/margin risk ahead, the analysts say. "We think there are still risks around Old Navy maintaining its historical growth and margin rates, especially with growing competition and nearing all-time margin highs," the analysts add, noting that they believe trends at Old Navy --which drives 60% of revenue and even more of profit, will be a key focus among investors near term. Shares gain 12%. (sabela.ojea@wsj.com; @sabelaojeaguix)

1027 ET - German Additional Tier 1 (AT1) bond spreads are wider than their eurozone peers, offering investors higher returns, ING's Suvi Kosonen says in a note. The wider spreads are due to lower debt ratings and riskier business models for some of the issuers, ING says. AT1 bonds are high-risk debt issued by banks for regulatory capital needs. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

1003 ET - U.S. median asking rent rose 0.4% year-over-year to $1,607 in February, the first increase in six months, according to Redfin. Rents climbed 0.6% month-over-month, but have been stable for 10 months, unlike the pandemic's 18% surge in 2021 and 4% drop in 2023. Supply and demand are now balanced, with new apartments meeting strong demand driven by high homebuying costs. However, slowing construction may push rents up next year, ending perks like free parking. Austin saw the biggest drop in median asking rent, down 9.4% to $1,404, followed by Salt Lake City and Jacksonville, FL., due to robust housing growth. Rents rose most in Cincinnati and Providence, RI. (chris.wack@wsj.com)

0855 ET - Gold futures rise as gold exchange-traded funds report inflows in February. Futures are up 0.15% at $2,930.90 a troy ounce. Gold ETFs recorded strong inflows of 100 metric tons in February, the third consecutive month of inflows, Commerzbank analysts say in a note. This ETF demand helped propel gold to a record high of $2,974.0 an ounce in late February, Commerzbank says. The inflows were mainly concentrated in the U.S., where the largest inflow in a single month since July 2020, was recorded. European ETFs, on the other hand, only recorded a small increase in holdings. China saw ETF inflows of around 20 tons, or an increase of around 19%--indicating a growing interest in gold ETFs among Chinese investors. Until now, most Chinese investment demand was dominated by gold bars and coins, Commerzbank adds. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

0420 ET - Just Group's stock remains set for continued growth in the U.K. annuity market helped by its balance sheet, J.P. Morgan Cazenove analysts Farooq Hanif and Bingdi Fan say in a note. Although the analysts don't expect material positive underlying earnings revisions for 2025 as results for 2024 were close to consensus, they do see an upgrade on Solvency II ratio this year given its strong consensus beat of around 10 percentage points, the analysts say. "We also believe consensus may revise its dividend growth expectations up from here given the dividend per share beat," the analysts say. Shares are down 14% at 140.20 pence but are up 58% over the past 12 months. (anthony.orunagoriainoff@dowjones.com)

0207 ET - Malaysian banks are likely to see moderate profit growth in 2025, supported by steady loan expansion and benign provisioning costs, RAM Ratings says in a note. Net interest margins are likely to remain stable as banks manage funding costs with policy rates remaining unchanged, it says. Loan growth is projected at 5.5%, the same pace as 2024, it reckons. While Malaysia's GDP is forecast to grow 4.0%-5.0%, compared with an expansion of 5.1% last year, risks from impending subsidy reforms and global uncertainties may dampen sentiment, it adds. RAM Ratings forecasts banks asset quality to remain strong, with impaired loan ratios at historical lows. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

0055 ET - BDO Unibank's earnings growth is likely to be supported by loan volumes, SB Equities' Katrine Eunice Dolatre says in a research report. The Philippine bank's income growth prospects are expected to be primarily be volume-driven, aided by branch expansion--targeting 100-120 new locations this year--and ongoing investments in productivity, the analyst says. The brokerage maintains its 2025-2026 gross loan forecasts for BDO Unibank at roughly 12%, in line with the bank's low-teens growth guidance for 2025. It keeps a buy rating on the stock but lowers the target price to PHP181.20 from PHP186.40 to reflect factors including a higher risk-free rate. Shares are 1.4% higher at PHP157.20. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

2112 ET - Singapore banks are likely to face a challenging year ahead, say RHB Research analysts in a report. Earnings could stay flat this year given the high base effects, they write. Banks could post a modest rise in net interest income and a moderation in noninterest income, due to the high base of other noninterest income, they say. However, fee growth should remain healthy, the analysts say. "With operating income growth slowing, we expect [Singapore] banks to keep a tighter rein on cost resulting in flat jaws," they say. RHB maintains an overweight rating on Singapore's banking sector.(amanda.lee@wsj.com)

data.title: Energy & Utilities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Energy and Utilities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1522 ET - Oil futures post losses for the week in which markets were hit by OPEC+ saying its output increases will go ahead in April, uncertainty over U.S. tariffs, and a bearish build in U.S. crude stocks. Despite today's 1% gain, WTI stretches its losing streak to seven weeks, settling at $67.04 a barrel. WTI "may be finding some support," says Alex Hodes of StoneX in a note. "Although the highs of the year are likely in the rearview, there appears to be more price risk to the upside than downside at current price levels." He sees bullish risk with reports of OPEC+ rethinking the announced production returns and potential disruption to Iranian supplies. Brent rises 1.3% to $70.36 a barrel, and is down 3.4% on the week. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1449 ET - The tariffs President Trump has aimed at Canada have made even more critical for the nation to connect its energy products to Asian markets, AltaGas CEO Vern Yu says. He tells investors during a conference call the trade war Trump has launched on Canada reinforced the need for the company's third export facility on the west coast. The company says its Ridley Island Energy Export Facility, which commenced construction last August, is on track for its targeted 2026 in-service date. Yu says that within AltaGas's midstream business, it delivered record global export volumes of more than 122,000 barrels per day of liquefied petroleum gas to Asia in the last year. (robb.stewart@wsj.com)

1337 ET - The number of rigs drilling for oil in the U.S. was unchanged this week at 486, or 18 fewer than a year ago, oil service company Baker Hughes reports. Natural gas rigs slipped by 1 to 101, and were down 14 on the year. In a report this week, the EIA noted a 32% drop in natural gas rigs over the past two years, led by declines in the Appalachia and Haynesville regions, coinciding with low natural gas prices and adoption of advanced drilling and completion technologies. Producers responded to low prices by drilling less and curtailing production which increased inventories of drilled but uncompleted wells. "If natural gas demand and prices continue to rise, producers could be in a better economic position to complete these wells, potentially allowing them to quickly increase production," the EIA said. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1232 ET - Brazil's state-controlled oil company Petrobras has the capability to keep paying "good dividends and even extraordinary dividends," in coming quarters, Suno Research's Joao Daronco writes. Petrobras reported last month a $2.8 billion 4Q net loss, with a 11% drop in revenues from the 3Q, in dollar terms. Except for non-recurring items, Petrobras would've had a $3.1 billion net profit last quarter, the company said. Daronco says Petrobras' capital structure remains healthy. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

0845 ET - Oil futures attempt a recovery after President Trump postpones import tariffs on a range of goods from Mexico and Canada for another month, easing one of the bearish factors behind the recent selloff that include OPEC+ plans to start unwinding cuts next month. "While the delay in tariffs has provided a brief sigh of relief, the market is still walking a tightrope between policy uncertainty and oversupply concerns," Rystad Energy's Mukesh Sahdev says in a note. "The tariff threats sent shockwaves through markets, not just because of potential trade disruptions, but also due to fears of retaliatory moves that could ripple through energy supply chains." WTI is up 1.5% at $67.34 a barrel, and Brent is up 1.5% at $70.52 a barrel.(anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1817 ET - Australia's filling-station operators and supermarkets face earnings headwinds from the availability elsewhere of cheaper illicit tobacco products, according to Jefferies analysts. They write in a note that recent earnings reports from Viva Energy, Ampol, Coles and Woolworths show tobacco sales falling by between 15% and 26%. They point out that, while gross margins on tobacco sales are low, there are little associated costs, so a drop in sales implies material operating deleverage. They think that Viva likely experienced a 12% drag on December-half Ebit from the lower sales. The other three companies have better managed the decline, the analysts add. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

data.title: Basic Materials Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Basic Materials. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0716 ET - Cleveland-Cliffs is launching a company-wide "Buy American" incentive for its nearly 30,000 employees. During calendar year 2025, any Cleveland-Cliffs employee who buys or leases a new American-built vehicle with substantial Cliffs' steel content will receive a $1,000 cash bonus in connection with the purchase. Vehicles produced in a plant outside of the U.S., or that use a significant amount of imported flat-rolled steel, will not qualify for the incentive. "We hope that other companies will follow suit and do their part to incentivize consumption of American-made products," says Lourenco Goncalves, Cliffs' chairman and CEO. (chris.wack@wsj.com)

0131 ET - Vale Indonesia's new nickel-ore sales initiatives are expected to be a key catalyst in 2025, Nomura's Ahmad Maghfur Usman and Verdhana Sekuritas analysts say in a research report. The nickel miner has disclosed improved feasibility for its upstream mine and high-pressure acid leach projects, with a planned gross investment of US$8.6 billion, the analysts note. Ore sales could drive a three-year revenue CAGR of 29%, according to the brokerage's estimates. The company's limonite ore will supply four new HPAL facilities set to come online by late 2026. Nomura upgrades the stock's rating to buy from neutral, maintaining a target price of IDR4,000.00. Shares are 10.6% higher at IDR3,140.00. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

2301 ET - Hyundai Steel may get an earnings boost from China's steel output reduction and stimulus efforts, NH Investment & Securities analysts Rhee Jae-kwang and S.Y. Lee write in a note. The South Korean steelmaker can benefit from a possible steel-sector recovery, as China's economic planning agency earlier this week said it would mandate steel production cuts to restructure the sector, the NH analysts say. The agency has yet to give specific reduction targets, but markets expect China to cut its steel output by 150 million tons by 2030, they note. NH raises its target price for the stock by 25% to KRW40,000 and keeps a buy rating. Shares are 8.0% higher at KRW31,650. (kwanwoo.jun@wsj.com)

2144 ET - SK IE Technology may report a narrower-than-expected loss this year as it secures more contracts, including a 291.40 billion won deal in February, Nomura analyst Cindy Park writes in a note. The South Korean lithium-battery separator maker's shipments are forecast to rise 97% in 2025 but fall short of helping it break even, Park says. She expects the company's annual net loss at 74 billion won, compared with her earlier projection of 107 billion won, after factoring in new contract wins and still uncertain electric-vehicle demand. Nomura cuts its target price on the stock by 25% to KRW30,000 and keeps a neutral rating. Shares are 0.2% higher at KRW27,100. (kwanwoo.jun@wsj.com)

2129 ET - Iron ore prices are higher in early Asia trade. China's economic planning agency has said it would mandate steel production cuts, ANZ Research analysts write in a note, adding that no specifics were given on the volume of cuts in the sector. Some state media reports indicate that steel mills in Shandong have been instructed to cut production by around 4 million tons this year, ANZ says. The weak domestic market has also led Chinese steel mills to target the export market, which has weighed on international prices, they say. The most-traded iron-ore contract on the Dalian Commodity Exchange gains 0.1% at CNY773.5/ton. (jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

1958 ET - Jefferies adds Barrick Gold to its North American shopping list for copper--an unconventional call, it acknowledges, given Barrick makes most of its money from the gold it mines. Analysts at the bank say they expect Barrick's attributable copper production to double to more than 450,000 metric tons annually and contribute roughly 25% of revenue later this decade. Copper accounts for about 10% of its revenue today. Barrick joins Capstone Copper, Ivanhoe Mines and Hudbay Minerals on Jefferies's list of preferred North American copper miners. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

1853 ET [Dow Jones]--Improvements in global softwood pulp market conditions are expected to continue into the second quarter, Canfor Pulp said, "as global supply dynamics adjust to new hardwood capacity and as pulp producer inventories normalize." However, the company said it is facing persistent challenges associated with the availability of economic fiber in British Columbia. "On the demand side, steady Chinese demand is anticipated to absorb these changes in supply," the company added. Canfor Pulp also said it is monitoring the trade situation between Canada and the U.S., with mitigation plans under way to mostly offset the impact of tariffs on U.S. shipments. The company said it is "well-positioned to respond to actual and potential tariffs." (stephen.nakrosis@wsj.com)

1849 ET - Rio Tinto has no need to raise equity, say Citi analysts Paul McTaggart and Ephrem Ravi. "So let's move on," they say in a client note. Rio Tinto's recent comments about a potential equity raise caught the market off guard and prompted a debate over whether the miner was seeking to rebalance shares in its dual-listed structure, strengthen its balance sheet, or have greater flexibility, say McTaggart and Ravi. "Even under downside scenarios, we think that Rio's balance sheet can handle commodity price volatility without new equity," they say. And, "if management believes Rio's shares are cheap, why dilute shareholders now?" Citi has a neutral rating at A$130 target on Rio Tinto. The stock is up 0.3% in Sydney at A$115.30. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

1657 ET - West African Resources' share price may have shot up 19% in two days after its annual result beat expectations, but Barrenjoey says the miner remains its top stock pick among West African gold companies. "One year forward earnings multiples are still eye watering," says analyst Richard Knights. The bank assumes West African Resources offers a free cash flow yield of 32% in 2026. "If Kiaka is delivered in the September quarter of 2025 as expected, we don't believe this discount will persist, even considering the market's concerns around sovereign risks in Burkina Faso," Barrenjoey says. Its price target rises 3.9%, to A$2.65/share. West African Resources ended Thursday at A$2.11. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

data.title: Auto & Transport Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering the Auto and Transport sector. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1507 ET - The Trump administration's push to shrink the government could hurt the travel industry, and the broader U.S. economy, Melius Research analysts say in a note. They point to United Airlines having recently said that its government business has weakened following the U.S. election in November. The analysts, however, note that airlines' exposure to government is relatively small, accounting for less than 2% of revenue at United, and about 1% of sales at Delta. "The question is whether the weakness within the government sector will ripple throughout other parts of the economy," the analysts write. "Hard to tell, but clearly there is growing uneasiness on how that might be contained." (connor.hart@wsj.com)

0937 ET - MDA Space says that M&A is a big part of the company's expansion plans, opening up new markets and access to new talent. CEO Mike Greenley says on an analyst call that he has specific acquisition strategies: to be more vertically integrated and to secure the supply chain, and to add more capabilities in other geographic areas. The executive says the company is particularly focused on the US and Europe, where there is strong demand from governments for aerospace products. Another part of the consideration is access to talent in new geographies that could support the business. (adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

0933 ET - MDA Space CEO Mike Greenley says the tariff situation is manageable for its business. On an analyst call, the executive says the aerospace company is actively engaged in dialogues with governments. "Tariffs come and tariffs go," the CEO says, but "when we look at our contracts, and we look at how tariffs work, we don't see a concern for us at this time." Part of the security comes from its customers, where about 90% of its business is from outside the U.S. The CEO also says the company's customers have the same perspective, and he notes that its new business pipeline remains "robust." (adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

0911 ET - MDA Space says that most of its supply chain is based outside of the US. On an analyst call, executives of the Canadian aerospace company, say that only about one-quarter of its supply chain is sourced in the U.S., while the rest is part of a larger global network. The company is still keeping an eye on the evolving US tariff situation, but says that it hasn't felt a dampening of demand for its products and services. The company's backlog as of the end of the quarter was C$4.39 billion, up from C$3.1 billion a year earlier. (adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

0756 ET - Canada's auto industry likely won't face a prolonged tariff situation, according to TD Cowen. In a report, analyst Brian Morrison says that the recent implementation then reversal of tariffs shows the complexity of the auto supply chain and how prolonged tariff duration would be negative to the U.S.'s own auto industry and economy. "This is not the intent of the Trump administration, in our view, and as such we anticipate in time to see modest changes favouring a gradual shift to U.S. content with limited disruption by H2/25," he says. (adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

0716 ET - Cleveland-Cliffs is launching a company-wide "Buy American" incentive for its nearly 30,000 employees. During calendar year 2025, any Cleveland-Cliffs employee who buys or leases a new American-built vehicle with substantial Cliffs' steel content will receive a $1,000 cash bonus in connection with the purchase. Vehicles produced in a plant outside of the U.S., or that use a significant amount of imported flat-rolled steel, will not qualify for the incentive. "We hope that other companies will follow suit and do their part to incentivize consumption of American-made products," says Lourenco Goncalves, Cliffs' chairman and CEO. (chris.wack@wsj.com)

0537 ET - Deutsche Post's valuation looks fair at current levels, but HSBC cautions that trade headwinds from tariff policies may dent the volume outlook. The company's share price was up 14% on the day of its results, on a higher buyback and better margins in the express business, despite cautious guidance and muted macro and business-to-business recovery, HSBC says. The capital markets day on April 3 is a potential catalyst as the company unveils its growth ambitions, the bank says. HSBC downgrades its rating to hold from buy and lifts its target price to 47 euros from 41 euros. Shares fall 3.9% to 42.55 euros. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0358 ET - Deutsche Post's guidance reassures as it has been set at a manageable level, Deutsche Bank analyst Andy Chu writes. The bank says that since roughly this time last year it had been concerned about the risk to management guidance and the lack of a turn in the freight cycle. "Today's results allow us to pivot our view as guidance has been de-risked and re-based down to more achievable levels." Full-year EBIT guidance is in line with the bank's estimates and the medium-term guidance now doesn't try to predict economic cycles, Chu says. The bank upgrades Deutsche Post to buy from hold and raises its target price to 50 euros from 36 euros. Shares fall 2.4% to 43.22 euros. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0325 ET - Deutsche Post's investment case has been strengthened by management actions to contain costs and boost growth, JPMorgan analysts write. The company delivered strong fourth-quarter EBIT, derisked forward guidance by targeting an EBIT level it is confident in reaching despite macro uncertainties, and outlined a cost-improvement plan. "While share price performance year to date has been strong, partly driven by sentiment around German equities, we think the fact that management is demonstrating it is taking action to ensure earnings growth resumes, will help the investment case." JPMorgan lifts its price target on the stock to 50 euros from 42.50 euros and reiterates an overweight rating. Shares fall 1.8% to 43.48 euros. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0239 ET - Ninebot is poised to strengthen its dominance in the high-end intelligent electric two-wheeler market, Daiwa Capital Markets analysts say in a research report. Initiating coverage with a buy rating and a target price of CNY70.00, the brokerage projects the company's market share in China's high-end segment will reach around 36% by 2027. Analysts cite the company's leadership in intelligent technology, strong brand awareness among young consumers and ongoing nationwide expansion of its distribution network. Daiwa forecasts EPS CAGR of 37% for Ninebot over 2025-2027. Shares closed 2.1% higher at CNY60.39. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

2148 ET - Airports of Thailand faces greater hit to FY 2025 earnings from the delay in concession payments, UOB Kay Hian analysts say in a research report. The Thai company this week clarified that King Power Duty Free's concession payment will be postponed from September 2024 to April 2025 and King Power Selection's concession payment will be delayed from September 2024 to February 2025. Management at its 1Q FY 2025 analysts' meeting said that KPD and KPS had requested to delay concession payments from August 2024 to February 2025. These changes could have an estimated THB2.1 billion negative impact on AOT's FY 2025 concession revenue, the analysts say. The brokerage cuts the stock's target price to THB49.00 from THB52.00 with unchanged hold rating. Shares closed at THB40.50 on Thursday. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

1919 ET - Napier Port measures up well against its chief listed rival in New Zealand for stock pickers, according to Forsyth Barr. "Napier Port is trading at a 50% discount to its larger sector peer, Port of Tauranga, which presents an attractive entry opportunity, not least given a robust medium-term earnings growth outlook," analyst Andy Bowley says. Napier Port this week said it's tracking toward the top end of its FY 2025 Ebitda guidance range. It also reiterated a key goal for return on capital. Napier Port is down 2.2% today, amid broad-based weakness among NZ stocks. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

data.title: Texas, New Mexico report increase in measles cases
data.description:
Key points:
  • Texas and New Mexico report 59 new measles cases
  • CDC says risk remains low due to immunization program
  • Lawmakers call for congressional hearing to discuss spread

Texas and New Mexico on Friday reported an additional 59 cases of measles for a total of 228 known infections in those states, where an outbreak that began in West Texas in late January has spread, causing the first U.S. measles deaths in a decade.

The U.S. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) said in its first health alert to physicians on the outbreak that the risk for widespread measles in the U.S. remained low due to robust immunization and surveillance programs and outbreak response capacity.

The agency said the Measles, Mumps, and Rubella (MMR) vaccine remains the most important tool for preventing measles, adding all U.S. residents born after 1957 should have either been vaccinated or have immunity from laboratory confirmation of the disease.

In Texas, measles cases grew to 198 as of March 7, up 39, according to state health department data. These included 30 new cases in Gaines County, which now has a total of 137. A week ago, the state reported the death from measles of an unvaccinated child who had no underlying health concerns.

In New Mexico, cases rose to 30 on Friday, up from 10 on Thursday, when the state also reported that an adult who had died tested positive for measles. Medical examiners were still investigating that case, its first measles-related death.

The largest measles outbreak in a decade has been the first major challenge for U.S. Health Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr., a longtime vaccine skeptic.

In a cabinet meeting last week, Kennedy initially downplayed news that a school-aged child had died of measles, calling such outbreaks ordinary and failing to mention the role of vaccination to prevent measles.

Over the weekend, Kennedy published an opinion piece on Fox News that promoted the role of vaccination, but also said vaccination was a personal choice and urged people to consult with their physician.

He has said that in addition to vaccines, the CDC has sent vitamin A for the outbreak, which has been shown to ease symptoms in people who are malnourished and vitamin A deficient.

The CDC said in its alert that doctors and public health officials should be vigilant for patients that show symptoms of measles such as fever and rash.

U.S. Representatives Frank Pallone of New Jersey, Diana DeGette of Colorado and Yvette Clarke of New York, all Democrats, called for a congressional oversight hearing to look into the rapidly spreading measles outbreak.

data.title: President Trump's Media Company Names Holding to Board to Replace McMahon
data.description:
By Sabela Ojea

Trump Media & Technology Group is adding former Congressman George Holding to its board of directors following the appointment of Linda McMahon as President Trump's secretary of education.

The company behind Truth Social on Friday said that its board voted in favor of Holding's appointment, who will take McMahon's seat.

McMahon stepped down as an independent director of the board upon her appointment as education secretary, the company said.

She was confirmed by the Senate on Monday.

Holding served as a Republican member of the U.S. Congress representing North Carolina from 2013 to 2021. Since then, he has worked as a managing director of government relations for Blackstone.

data.title: Dow Trims Losses to End a Tough Week — WSJ
data.description:
By Matt Wirz

An ugly week on Wall Street ended with tumultuous trading Friday as major stock indexes seesawed before finishing slightly higher. The S&P 500 index dropped 3.1% for the week, its biggest one-week decline since Sept. 6.

Investors turned defensive out of concern about the Trump administration's unpredictable tariff policies and how they would affect economic growth. That fueled sharp declines in technology and financial stocks, which had pushed indexes to record highs earlier this year.

"So much is happening from a news headline perspective," said Alex Coffey, a senior trading and derivatives strategist at Schwab. "There have been several moments in the past two weeks when we've had pretty sizable bounces in some megacap tech names, but every single time they've been getting slammed back down."

U.S. stocks fell initially on Friday when the monthly jobs report showed hiring picked up a touch in February, with 151,000 jobs added, but slightly under expectations. The unemployment rate crept up to 4.1%, while it had been forecast to hold steady.

Indexes rebounded back to positive territory after Federal Reserve Chairman Jerome Powell delivered remarks about taking a wait-and-see approach to interest rates. Indexes still finished the week significantly lower.

Nvidia, a big beneficiary of artificial-intelligence euphoria, lost about $1 trillion of market capitalization since its stock peaked at the start of the year. Stock prices for Goldman Sachs, which recently reported a doubling of profits, sank about 17% over the same period. Tesla shares lost all of their postelection gains for the first time on Friday.

The bearish pivot pushed indexes down below thresholds that many traders use as signals to reduce risk exposure. The Dow Jones Industrial dipped below its 200-day moving average on Friday for the first time since November 2023, before rebounding and closing above the threshold.

"That puts on more pressure from automatic selling," said Keith Lerner, co-chief investment officer at Truist Advisory Services. "We're looking for more of those give-up trades and it can happen quickly."

U.S. Treasury yields edged higher, with the 10-year yield moving up to 4.317%. The greenback also weakened, with the WSJ Dollar Index dropping to its lowest level since the days after President Trump's election in November.

Bearish sentiment crept into credit markets as the yield premium, or spread, that investors demanded to own corporate bonds rather than U.S. Treasurys rose from nearly record lows in February.

"Small cracks are forming," Bank of America analyst Neha Khoda wrote in a research report Friday. "Credit has finally started to respond to volatility in equities and rates."

Stock traders are rotating out of tech and financial investments that were popular just weeks ago because they tend to outperform in periods of strong economic growth. Instead they are turning to perceived havens of safety.

European stocks, which have traded at lower multiples than their U.S. counterparts, were among the biggest gainers of the week. The German government's decision to boost borrowing for defense spending and to reboot its flagging economy prompted some U.S. investors to bargain hunt on the Continent. An iShares exchange-traded fund focused on German stocks was one of the top performers last week, gaining 6.27%.

In the U.S., McDonald's stock gained about 4.2% for the week. The fast-food restaurant chain is viewed as a strong performer in times of economic contraction when budget-conscious consumers turn away from more expensive options.

Write to Matt Wirz at matthieu.wirz@wsj.com

data.title: S&P 500 Starts March With Weekly Slide Amid Tariff Worries, Weaker-Than-Expected Jobs
data.description:
The Standard & Poor's 500 index fell 3.1% this week, pushing the market benchmark into the red for the year amid worries about tariffs and a weaker-than-expected February jobs report.

The S&P 500 ended Friday's session at 5,770.20, marking its third consecutive down week. The index has dropped 1.9% from where it ended 2024.

Investors are worried about economic impacts from the Trump administration's tariff policies as well as job cuts. Earlier this week, the administration said it would be placing 25% tariffs on Mexico and Canada, though it later delayed some of the tariffs by a month. Meanwhile, its Department of Government Efficiency has been cutting jobs across the government.

The February employment report showed nonfarm payrolls rose by 151,000, which was smaller than the increase of 160,000 jobs expected in a survey compiled by Bloomberg. The unemployment rate rose to 4.1% in February from 4% in January. No change had been expected.

All but one sector of the S&P 500 fell this week. Financials had the largest percentage drop, sliding 5.9%, followed by a 5.4% decline in consumer discretionary, a 3.8% loss in energy and a 3.4% decrease in technology.

KKR was hit hardest in the financial sector, falling 15%. The company priced 45 million shares or $2.25 billion worth of its 6.25% Series D mandatory convertible preferred stock at $50.00 per share.

Among consumer discretionary stocks, shares of Best Buy fell 12% as Chief Executive Corie Barry warned of price rises due to tariffs imposed by President Donald Trump. Best Buy also issued a full-year earnings outlook that fell short of Wall Street estimates at the midpoint.

The energy sector's drop came as crude oil futures also fell on the week. Diamondback Energy was among the decliners, losing 12% as the company said it priced a debt offering to raise $1.2 billion.

In technology, Hewlett Packard Enterprise shares shed 20% as the company issued fiscal Q2 profit guidance that trailed analysts' projections, citing industry-wide "uncertainty" from the current US tariff policy. Hewlett Packard Enterprise also shared plans to achieve roughly $350 million in run-rate cost savings by fiscal 2027 through job cuts. It plans to reduce its workforce by 5%, or about 2,500 employees, over the next 12 to 18 months.

Health care was the lone sector in positive territory for the week, edging up 0.2%.

Moderna was the sector's best performer, with its shares jumping 15% as a German court ruled that Pfizer (PFE) and its partner BioNTech (BNTX) infringed a COVID-19 vaccine patent owned by Moderna, according to a Reuters report. The court ordered Pfizer and BioNTech to disclose earnings related to the patent's use and pay a compensation to Moderna, according to the report.

Next week, earnings reports are expected from companies including Oracle (ORCL) and Adobe (ADBE).

Economic data will feature the February consumer price index and producer price index, two closely watched inflation readings.

data.title: Fairfax Financial Names Amy Sherk CFO; Jennifer Allen to Move to Chief Business Officer

Fairfax Financial Holdings (FFH) on Friday said Amy Sherk will become the chief financial officer, while Jennifer Allen, who has held the role since 2019, will move to chief business officer, effective Monday.

Sherk, with over 20 years at Fairfax, most recently served as CFO of Fairfax India (FIH-U.TO). Debbie Chalkley, with 13 years at Fairfax, will become CFO of Fairfax India starting Monday, the company added.

"Amy has been outstanding in her numerous roles with Fairfax companies over the past 20 years and is the natural successor to Jenn, just as she was at Fairfax India. We know from our long relationship with Amy that she has the knowledge, commitment and experience to seamlessly assume the CFO position at Fairfax," Chief Executive Prem Watsa said.

data.title: US dollar sags after weaker-than-expected jobs data, Fed's Powell comments
data.description:
Key points:
  • US payrolls rise to 151,000 in February, lower than expected
  • US wage earnings growth slows
  • US rate futures price in three rate cuts in 2025
  • Euro on pace for best weekly gain in 16 years
  • Fed's Powell says cenbank in no rush to cut rates

The U.S. dollar dropped to multi-month lows against the euro and yen and fell versus most currencies on Friday after data showed the labor market in the world's largest economy slowed last month, creating fewer jobs than expected.

The report suggested that the Federal Reserve remained on track to cut interest rates multiple times this year. U.S. rate futures on Friday priced in 78 basis points (bps) of easing this year following the nonfarm payrolls report, or about three rate cuts of 25 bps each, according to LSEG calculations. The first rate reduction is likely to resume in June.

Fed Chair Jerome Powell, in prepared remarks to the University of Chicago School of Business, did not really say anything new, repeating comments during his testimony before Congress and his press conference after the Fed rate decision in January.

He said on Friday the U.S. central bank will be in no rush to cut rates while it waits for more clarity on how policies of the new Trump administration affect the economy.

The euro, on the other hand, continued its winning ways, poised for its best week in 16 years with a gain of 4.5% against the dollar, boosted by Germany's game-changing fiscal reforms. It hit another four-month peak of $1.0888 after the jobs data. It last traded at $1.0845, up 0.6% .

Against the Japanese currency, the greenback was flat on the day at 147.99 yen , after earlier falling to a five-month low of 146.94 yen.

The dollar extended its losses after data showed nonfarm payrolls increased by 151,000 jobs last month after rising by a downwardly revised 125,000 in January. Economists polled by Reuters had forecast payrolls gaining 160,000 jobs after a previously reported 143,000 rise in January.

The dollar index , which measures the greenback's value versus six major currencies, has fallen 3.5% this week, on track for its worst weekly performance since November 2022. It fell 0.4% on Friday to 103.81, after earlier sliding to its lowest since early November.

Natalia Lojevsky, managing director, at CIFC Asset Management, with $44 billion in assets under management thinks "the softer average hourly earnings is probably a relief for the Fed, who continue to evaluate inflationary pressures both in the labor market and broader economy as a result of the yet uncertain trade and tariff policy."

Last month's average hourly earnings, a measure of wage inflation, moderated to a 0.3% rise, from an 0.5% increase in January.

Overall, it has been a volatile week for the currency market, driven mainly by U.S. trade and economic growth uncertainties and a pivotal development in Europe as its largest economy abandoned its fiscal constraints to boost spending and revive growth.

Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said on Friday the U.S. economy may slow as it transitions away from public spending towards more private spending, calling it a "detox period" needed to reach a more sustainable equilibrium.

Another reprieve of levies aimed at Mexico and Canada announced by President Donald Trump on Thursday offered little relief to whiplashed markets. The exemption expires on April 2 when Trump said he will impose reciprocal tariffs on all U.S. trading partners.

On Friday, Fed Chair Powell said a one-time jump in prices due to tariffs does not need a monetary policy response .

"The Fed is going to be very conservative on what they say until we know whether or not the tariffs are tactical or strategic, and if they do turn out to be strategic, they're still going to wait until the hard data demonstrates both that inflation is low, and the economy is in recession before they really do very much," said Tony Roth, chief investment officer at Wilmington Trust Investment Advisors.

The greenback rose 0.4% against the Canadian dollar to C$1.4362, but slipped versus the Mexican peso to 20.259 pesos.

In cryptocurrencies, both bitcoin and ether fell in the midst of a summit of industry leaders at the White House on Friday, which is likely to focus on Trump's plans to build a strategic reserve containing bitcoin and four other coins.

Trump on Thursday signed an executive order to establish the strategic bitcoin reserve , directing the secretaries of Treasury and Commerce to develop "budget-neutral strategies" for acquiring additional bitcoin that have no "incremental costs" on taxpayers.

Bitcoin was last down 2.8% at $87,030.54 , while ether last changed hands at $2,150 , down 2.6%.

data.title: Wall St ends higher after Fed chief's comments, but posts big weekly loss
data.description:
Key points:
  • Hewlett Packard slumps after dour second-quarter forecasts
  • Costco falls after retailer misses estimates
  • Nonfarm payrolls increase by 151,000 in February

U.S. stocks finished higher on Friday, rebounding from early declines after Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell said the economy was "in a good place," but uncertainty about U.S. trade policy led to Wall Street's biggest weekly decline in months.

Powell said the central bank will not be quick to cut interest rates and echoed concerns about President Donald Trump's policies.

Markets have been roiled this week by uncertainty about Trump's tariff decisions on imported goods from Canada, Mexico and China.

The benchmark S&P 500 finished with its biggest weekly loss since September. The S&P 500 and the Nasdaq also registered their third straight week of declines, the longest losing streak since mid-July and early August last year.

Powell said the Fed will take a cautious approach to monetary policy easing, adding the economy currently "continues to be in a good place".

"Powell is echoing what the rest of us feel: unease that while the adjustments made by the administration may well work and put the country on better financial footing, the speed and whipsaw-like nature of the change makes it difficult to predict and to plan around," said Jamie Cox, managing partner at Harris Financial Group in Richmond, Virginia. "So, the best action when that occurs is to sit and wait."

Stocks fell in choppy early trade, but rebounded after Powell's comments. The three main indexes ended the week lower, with In the previous session, the Nasdaq confirmed a 10% drop from its December all-time high.

Utilities , energy , technology and industrials were the biggest gainers among the S&P 500's 11 main sectors. Consumer discretionary , financials and consumer staples were the biggest drag.

The Dow Jones Industrial Average rose 222.64 points, or 0.52%, to 42,801.72, the S&P 500 gained 31.68 points, or 0.55%, to 5,770.20 and the Nasdaq Composite gained 126.97 points, or 0.70%, to 18,196.22.

For the week, the S&P 500 ended down 3.1%, the Nasdaq declined 3.45%, and the Dow fell 2.37%. The Russell 2000 Small Cap index fell 3.86%.

Data early on Friday showed U.S. job growth picked up in February from the previous month. However, thousands of recent firings of federal workers were not reflected in the data.

Unemployment ticked up to 4.1%, adding to worries about the economy's resilience. Morgan Stanley and Goldman Sachs have lowered their growth forecasts for the economy.

"This is a growth scare," said Adam Hetts, portfolio manager at Janus Henderson Investors. "This is what it feels like to go from a no-landing to a soft-landing environment and it's unpleasant. It involves a spate of unpleasant economic data, and the primary driver being weaker consumer spending."

On Thursday, Trump offered a four-week reprieve on tariffs he imposed on imports from Canada and Mexico that fall under a free-trade pact. The U.S. remains in a trade war with China.

Reciprocal trade barriers and other duties are expected to take effect in the following weeks.

Hewlett Packard Enterprise slumped 12% after saying its annual profit forecast would be hit by U.S. tariffs.

Costco fell 6% after the retailer missed Wall Street estimates on quarterly earnings as merchandise costs increased.

Broadcom gained 8.6% after the chipmaker assuaged investor worries about artificial intelligence infrastructure demand with a strong second-quarter forecast.

Advancing issues outnumbered decliners by a 1.35-to-1 ratio on the NYSE. There were 92 new highs and 136 new lows on the NYSE.

The S&P 500 posted 8 new 52-week highs and 13 new lows while the Nasdaq Composite recorded 28 new highs and 159 new lows.

About 16.92 billion worth of shares were traded across U.S. exchanges, compared with the 20-day moving average of 16.23 billion shares.
